words.simpleMeaning: to do a job or task

sentences.daily: I work from 9 AM to 6 PM.
sentences.office: She works in the finance department.
sentences.interview: I have worked in IT for three years.
sentences.business: Our team works efficiently to meet deadlines.
sentences.formal: The committee works to improve public health.
sentences.informal: He works like a machine — never stops!

words.usageNote: 'Work' as a noun means the job itself. 'Work' as a verb means doing the job. Don't say 'I do work' when you mean 'I work'.

words.simpleMeaning: to gain knowledge or skill

sentences.daily: I learn something new every day.
sentences.office: She learns new software quickly.
sentences.interview: I learn from my mistakes and improve.
sentences.business: Our employees learn through regular training.
sentences.formal: Students learn best in interactive environments.
sentences.informal: He never learns from his mistakes!

words.usageNote: Both 'learned' and 'learnt' are correct as past tense. 'Learned' is more common in American English, 'learnt' in British English.

words.simpleMeaning: to say words, to talk

sentences.daily: I speak Hindi and English.
sentences.office: Please speak clearly in the meeting.
sentences.interview: I speak confidently about my achievements.
sentences.business: Our CEO speaks at international conferences.
sentences.formal: The ambassador speaks on behalf of the nation.
sentences.informal: Just speak up! Don't be shy.

words.usageNote: Use 'speak' for languages (speak English) and formal situations. Use 'talk' for conversations. Don't confuse with 'say' which is used for exact words.

words.simpleMeaning: to know the meaning of something

sentences.daily: Do you understand what I am saying?
sentences.office: I understand the project requirements completely.
sentences.interview: I understand complex technical concepts easily.
sentences.business: We need to understand customer needs.
sentences.formal: The court understands the gravity of the situation.
sentences.informal: I just don't understand why he did that!

words.usageNote: 'Understand' is a state verb — do not use it in continuous form. Say 'I understand' NOT 'I am understanding'.

words.simpleMeaning: to assist someone or make something easier

sentences.daily: Can you help me carry these bags?
sentences.office: She helps new employees settle in.
sentences.interview: I always help my team members when they need it.
sentences.business: Our product helps businesses save time.
sentences.formal: The government helps citizens through welfare schemes.
sentences.informal: Thanks for helping me out, buddy!

words.usageNote: 'Help' can be followed by infinitive with or without 'to': 'Help me do this' or 'Help me to do this' — both are correct.

words.simpleMeaning: to require something, to must have

sentences.daily: I need water after exercise.
sentences.office: The project needs more resources.
sentences.interview: I need challenges to grow professionally.
sentences.business: Businesses need to adapt to survive.
sentences.formal: The proposal needs further review before approval.
sentences.informal: I really need a break right now!

words.usageNote: 'Need' as a main verb: 'I need to go.' 'Need' as a modal: 'You need not worry.' In questions: 'Do you need help?' (not 'Need you help?')

words.simpleMeaning: to have information about something

sentences.daily: I know the way to the station.
sentences.office: She knows all the company policies.
sentences.interview: I know Python, Java, and SQL.
sentences.business: We know our customers' preferences well.
sentences.formal: The judge knows the law thoroughly.
sentences.informal: You know what? He was right all along!

words.usageNote: 'Know' is a state verb — never use in continuous form. Say 'I know him' NOT 'I am knowing him'.

words.simpleMeaning: to use your mind to consider something

sentences.daily: I think the weather will be good today.
sentences.office: Please think before you send that email.
sentences.interview: I think critically to solve complex problems.
sentences.business: We think long-term when making decisions.
sentences.formal: The committee thinks the proposal requires revision.
sentences.informal: I think she likes you, honestly!

words.usageNote: 'Think' for opinions: 'I think it is correct.' 'Think about/of' for consideration: 'I am thinking about the problem.'

words.simpleMeaning: to look at written words and understand them

sentences.daily: I read the newspaper every morning.
sentences.office: She reads all client emails carefully.
sentences.interview: I read industry reports to stay updated.
sentences.business: Our team reads competitor analysis monthly.
sentences.formal: Please read the terms and conditions carefully.
sentences.informal: I read this book in one night — so good!

words.usageNote: Present and past tense are spelled the same ('read') but pronounced differently: present = /riːd/ (reed), past = /red/ (red).

words.simpleMeaning: to put words on paper or screen

sentences.daily: I write in my diary every night.
sentences.office: She writes project proposals.
sentences.interview: I write clear and concise reports.
sentences.business: Our team writes marketing content.
sentences.formal: Please write your feedback on the form.
sentences.informal: Just write whatever comes to your mind!

words.usageNote: Note the irregular forms: write → wrote → written. 'Write to someone' (for letters/emails), 'write about something' (for topics).

words.simpleMeaning: to move toward a place or person

sentences.daily: Please come home by 8 PM.
sentences.office: She comes to work by metro.
sentences.interview: I can come for the interview on Monday.
sentences.business: New opportunities come with hard work.
sentences.formal: The delegation comes from five different countries.
sentences.informal: Come on! We are getting late!

words.usageNote: Use 'come' when moving toward the speaker. Use 'go' when moving away from the speaker.

words.simpleMeaning: to move away from a place

sentences.daily: I go to the market on Saturdays.
sentences.office: She goes to client meetings every week.
sentences.interview: I am willing to go the extra mile.
sentences.business: Our sales team goes to different cities.
sentences.formal: The proposal goes to the board for approval.
sentences.informal: Let us go before the traffic gets bad!

words.usageNote: Irregular verb: go → went → gone. 'Gone' is used with 'have': 'He has gone home.' 'Went' is simple past: 'He went home.'

words.simpleMeaning: to hand something to someone

sentences.daily: Please give me a glass of water.
sentences.office: The manager gives feedback weekly.
sentences.interview: I give my 100% to every project.
sentences.business: We give our clients the best solutions.
sentences.formal: The company gives annual bonuses to employees.
sentences.informal: Give me five minutes and I will be ready!

words.usageNote: Pattern: 'give something to someone' or 'give someone something'. Both are correct: 'Give the book to him' or 'Give him the book'.

words.simpleMeaning: to get hold of something and carry it

sentences.daily: I take the bus to college.
sentences.office: She takes notes during meetings.
sentences.interview: I take initiative in my projects.
sentences.business: The company takes responsibility seriously.
sentences.formal: Please take a seat and wait.
sentences.informal: Take it easy! It is not that serious.

words.usageNote: 'Take time' means it requires time. 'Take a break' means to rest. Many phrasal verbs: take off, take over, take care, take place.

words.simpleMeaning: to create or produce something

sentences.daily: My mother makes delicious food.
sentences.office: She makes detailed reports.
sentences.interview: I make sure every deadline is met.
sentences.business: We make innovative products for the market.
sentences.formal: The team makes strategic decisions.
sentences.informal: Let us make a deal — you cook, I clean!

words.usageNote: Difference: 'Make' = create from scratch (make tea, make a plan). 'Do' = perform an activity (do homework, do exercise).

words.simpleMeaning: to get something by paying money

sentences.daily: I buy vegetables from the local market.
sentences.office: She buys office supplies every month.
sentences.interview: I bought online courses to improve my skills.
sentences.business: We buy raw materials from domestic suppliers.
sentences.formal: The organization buys equipment through tender process.
sentences.informal: I just bought the new iPhone — it is amazing!

words.usageNote: Irregular verb: buy → bought → bought. 'Buy from' a person/place, 'buy for' a purpose/person.

words.simpleMeaning: to exchange something for money

sentences.daily: He sells vegetables near my house.
sentences.office: Our team sells software solutions.
sentences.interview: I worked in sales and sold insurance products.
sentences.business: We sell our products in 20 countries.
sentences.formal: The company sells shares on the stock exchange.
sentences.informal: She sold her car for way too low a price!

words.usageNote: Irregular verb: sell → sold → sold. 'Sell at' a price, 'sell to' a customer, 'sell in' a market.

words.simpleMeaning: to make something no longer closed

sentences.daily: The shop opens at 9 AM.
sentences.office: She opens all emails in the morning.
sentences.interview: I am open to new challenges and learning.
sentences.business: We plan to open a new branch next year.
sentences.formal: The ceremony opens with the national anthem.
sentences.informal: Open the door! It is too hot in here.

words.usageNote: As adjective: 'The shop is open.' As verb: 'She opens the shop.' As adverb in phrase: 'in the open air'.

words.simpleMeaning: to shut something or end something

sentences.daily: Please close the window — it is raining.
sentences.office: She closes all pending tickets before leaving.
sentences.interview: I close deals effectively in sales.
sentences.business: We need to close this contract by Friday.
sentences.formal: The meeting closes with a summary of decisions.
sentences.informal: Close the door quietly, the baby is sleeping!

words.usageNote: As verb (verb): /kloʊz/ — 'Please close the door.' As adjective: /kloʊs/ — 'The shop is close to my home.' Different pronunciation!

words.simpleMeaning: to begin something

sentences.daily: I start my day with exercise.
sentences.office: The meeting starts at 10 AM sharp.
sentences.interview: I started my career as a junior developer.
sentences.business: We start every project with a planning phase.
sentences.formal: The academic year starts in June.
sentences.informal: Let us start already! We are waiting for you!

words.usageNote: 'Start' and 'begin' are similar but 'start' is more common in spoken English. 'Start' also refers to machines: 'Start the car.'

words.simpleMeaning: to no longer do something or move

sentences.daily: The bus stops near my house.
sentences.office: Please stop sending unnecessary emails.
sentences.interview: I stopped my previous job to pursue higher studies.
sentences.business: We stopped the old process and adopted automation.
sentences.formal: The government stopped the project due to funding issues.
sentences.informal: Stop talking and just do it!

words.usageNote: 'Stop doing' = permanently quit. 'Stop to do' = pause in order to do something else. 'I stopped smoking.' vs 'I stopped to drink water.'

words.simpleMeaning: to cause something to go to a place

sentences.daily: I send messages to my family every day.
sentences.office: She sends reports to the manager on Monday.
sentences.interview: I sent my resume to 20 companies.
sentences.business: We send invoices within 24 hours.
sentences.formal: The ministry sends official notices by post.
sentences.informal: Send me the photo when you get home!

words.usageNote: Irregular verb: send → sent → sent. 'Send to' a person, 'send by' a method (email, post). 'Send for' = ask someone to come.

words.simpleMeaning: to get something that was sent to you

sentences.daily: I received a gift from my friend.
sentences.office: She receives client calls daily.
sentences.interview: I received a scholarship for my studies.
sentences.business: We receive payments within 30 days.
sentences.formal: The president receives the award on behalf of the nation.
sentences.informal: Did you receive my message? I sent it an hour ago!

words.usageNote: 'Receive' is more formal than 'get'. In everyday speech, 'get' is more common: 'Did you get my email?' In business, 'receive' is preferred.

words.simpleMeaning: to give money for something

sentences.daily: I pay the rent on the first of every month.
sentences.office: The company pays salaries on the 25th.
sentences.interview: My previous company paid me well.
sentences.business: We pay suppliers on time to maintain good relations.
sentences.formal: Employees are paid according to their grade.
sentences.informal: I will pay you back tomorrow, I promise!

words.usageNote: Irregular verb: pay → paid → paid. 'Pay for' something = give money for it. 'Pay attention' and 'pay a visit' are fixed phrases.

words.simpleMeaning: to do something with a tool or thing

sentences.daily: I use my phone for everything.
sentences.office: She uses Excel for data analysis.
sentences.interview: I use agile methodology in my projects.
sentences.business: We use advanced tools to improve productivity.
sentences.formal: The organization uses renewable energy sources.
sentences.informal: Can I use your charger? Mine is dead!

words.usageNote: As noun: /juːs/ — 'What is the use of this?' As verb: /juːz/ — 'Please use the pen.' 'Used to' = past habit: 'I used to smoke but I quit.'

words.simpleMeaning: to discover or locate something

sentences.daily: I cannot find my keys.
sentences.office: She finds solutions to every problem.
sentences.interview: I find creative ways to handle challenges.
sentences.business: We need to find new customers.
sentences.formal: The investigation finds no evidence of fraud.
sentences.informal: I finally found a good restaurant near here!

words.usageNote: Irregular verb: find → found → found. 'Find out' = discover information. 'Find it easy/difficult' = consider it to be easy/difficult.

words.simpleMeaning: to have something and not give it away

sentences.daily: Please keep this room clean.
sentences.office: She keeps records of all transactions.
sentences.interview: I keep myself updated with industry trends.
sentences.business: We keep costs low to maximize profit.
sentences.formal: The committee keeps minutes of every meeting.
sentences.informal: Keep it secret — do not tell anyone!

words.usageNote: Irregular verb: keep → kept → kept. 'Keep doing' = continue doing. Many phrasal verbs: keep up, keep on, keep out, keep in touch.

words.simpleMeaning: to go away from a place or person

sentences.daily: I leave home at 8 AM every day.
sentences.office: She leaves the office at 7 PM.
sentences.interview: I left my previous company for better growth.
sentences.business: We leave nothing to chance in our planning.
sentences.formal: The session leaves room for further discussion.
sentences.informal: Just leave it — stop thinking about it!

words.usageNote: Irregular verb: leave → left → left. 'Leave' for places. 'Leave someone/something' = abandon. 'Leave a message' = write/record a message.

words.simpleMeaning: to make an effort to do something

sentences.daily: I always try to be on time.
sentences.office: She tries to finish work before the deadline.
sentences.interview: I try to find innovative solutions.
sentences.business: We try to exceed customer expectations.
sentences.formal: The team tries to maintain quality standards.
sentences.informal: Just try it once! You will love it!

words.usageNote: 'Try to do' = attempt (may or may not succeed). 'Try doing' = experiment with a method. 'Try on' = wear to check fit.

words.simpleMeaning: to let someone see something

sentences.daily: Let me show you the way.
sentences.office: She shows her work in weekly presentations.
sentences.interview: My portfolio shows my design skills.
sentences.business: Our results show 30% growth this year.
sentences.formal: The data shows a clear upward trend.
sentences.informal: Show me your new phone! I heard it is great.

words.usageNote: Pattern: 'show someone something' or 'show something to someone'. 'Show off' = display proudly. 'Show up' = arrive.

words.simpleMeaning: to inform someone about something

sentences.daily: Please tell me the time.
sentences.office: She tells the team about new updates.
sentences.interview: Tell me about yourself is always the first question.
sentences.business: We tell clients about our progress regularly.
sentences.formal: The report tells us about the performance gaps.
sentences.informal: Tell me everything! I want to know all the details!

words.usageNote: Irregular verb: tell → told → told. Always 'tell someone something' — 'tell' needs a person after it. Don't say 'tell about me' — say 'tell me about'.

words.simpleMeaning: to request information or something

sentences.daily: May I ask you a question?
sentences.office: She asks for feedback from her team.
sentences.interview: You can ask me anything about my experience.
sentences.business: We ask clients to fill a satisfaction survey.
sentences.formal: The committee asks applicants to submit documents.
sentences.informal: Just ask him! He will not bite you!

words.usageNote: 'Ask someone something' or 'ask someone about something'. 'Ask for' = request. 'Ask someone out' = invite on a date.

words.simpleMeaning: to respond to a question or request

sentences.daily: Please answer the phone.
sentences.office: She answers client queries within one hour.
sentences.interview: I answered all interview questions confidently.
sentences.business: We answer all complaints within 24 hours.
sentences.formal: The company answered the regulatory inquiry.
sentences.informal: Why are you not answering my calls?!

words.usageNote: 'Answer' the phone/door/question (no preposition). 'Reply to' an email/message. Both mean responding, but usage differs.

words.simpleMeaning: to come together with someone

sentences.daily: I meet my friends every weekend.
sentences.office: She meets clients in the conference room.
sentences.interview: Nice to meet you — I am Rahul.
sentences.business: We meet the project deadline every time.
sentences.formal: The delegates meet to discuss trade agreements.
sentences.informal: Let us meet for coffee tomorrow!

words.usageNote: Irregular verb: meet → met → met. 'Meet someone' = encounter for first time. 'Meet with someone' = have a planned meeting. 'Meet a deadline' = complete on time.

words.simpleMeaning: to become part of a group or organization

sentences.daily: Please join us for dinner tonight.
sentences.office: She joined the company three years ago.
sentences.interview: I want to join your organization because of its values.
sentences.business: Two companies joined to form a new entity.
sentences.formal: The country joins the international climate agreement.
sentences.informal: Come join us! The more the merrier!

words.usageNote: 'Join' a club/team/company. 'Join in' = participate in an activity already happening. 'Join' does not need 'to': say 'join the team' not 'join to the team'.

words.simpleMeaning: to stay in one place until something happens

sentences.daily: I wait for the bus every morning.
sentences.office: Please wait while I connect your call.
sentences.interview: I waited patiently for the interview result.
sentences.business: We do not wait for problems — we prevent them.
sentences.formal: Passengers are requested to wait in the designated area.
sentences.informal: Wait! I almost forgot my phone!

words.usageNote: 'Wait for someone/something'. 'Wait on someone' = serve at a table (restaurant context). 'Cannot wait' means very excited about something.

words.simpleMeaning: to phone someone or shout to get attention

sentences.daily: I call my mother every evening.
sentences.office: Please call me if there is any problem.
sentences.interview: We will call you after checking your documents.
sentences.business: Our team calls potential clients daily.
sentences.formal: The court calls the next witness.
sentences.informal: Call me when you reach! I will worry otherwise.

words.usageNote: 'Call someone' = phone them. 'Call someone something' = give them a name/title. 'Call off' = cancel. 'Call back' = return a call.

words.simpleMeaning: to examine or look at something carefully

sentences.daily: I check my phone first thing in the morning.
sentences.office: She checks all documents before submitting.
sentences.interview: I always check my work for errors.
sentences.business: We check quality at every stage of production.
sentences.formal: The auditor checks the financial records.
sentences.informal: Check this out — it is incredible!

words.usageNote: 'Check' something = look at it. 'Check in' = register at hotel/airport. 'Check out' = look at something interesting (informal). 'Check on' = see if someone is okay.

words.simpleMeaning: to control or handle something successfully

sentences.daily: I manage my time carefully.
sentences.office: She manages a team of 15 people.
sentences.interview: I managed a project worth 50 lakhs.
sentences.business: We manage risk through diversification.
sentences.formal: The director manages the entire operations.
sentences.informal: I somehow managed to finish everything on time!

words.usageNote: 'Manage to do' = succeed in doing something difficult. 'Manage someone' = supervise. 'Manager' = the person who manages.

words.simpleMeaning: to make something better

sentences.daily: I want to improve my English.
sentences.office: She improves processes to save time.
sentences.interview: I continuously improve my technical skills.
sentences.business: We improve customer experience every quarter.
sentences.formal: The scheme aims to improve public health.
sentences.informal: Just keep practicing and you will improve for sure!

words.usageNote: 'Improve' something = make it better. 'Improve on' = do better than a previous result. 'Room for improvement' = still can be made better.

words.simpleMeaning: having great value or significance

sentences.daily: Sleep is important for good health.
sentences.office: Punctuality is important in a professional setting.
sentences.interview: Communication skills are important for this role.
sentences.business: Customer feedback is important for growth.
sentences.formal: It is important to comply with safety regulations.
sentences.informal: The most important thing is that you tried!

words.usageNote: 'Important' describes things with great significance. 'Important for' (a purpose), 'important to' (a person). Don't say 'more important' say 'more importantly' in sentences.

words.simpleMeaning: not the same as something else

sentences.daily: Today is different from yesterday.
sentences.office: Each project is different in nature.
sentences.interview: I bring a different perspective to the team.
sentences.business: We offer different packages for different needs.
sentences.formal: Different regions have different cultural practices.
sentences.informal: You look different today — new haircut?

words.usageNote: 'Different from' is standard. 'Different than' is used in American English. 'Different to' is used in British English. All are acceptable.

words.simpleMeaning: able to be done or achieved

sentences.daily: Is it possible to change my appointment?
sentences.office: Please send the report as soon as possible.
sentences.interview: Is it possible to know the salary range?
sentences.business: We make the impossible possible.
sentences.formal: It is possible that the results may vary.
sentences.informal: There is no way that is possible — are you serious?!

words.usageNote: 'Possible' — it can happen. 'Possibly' — maybe, perhaps (adverb). 'As soon as possible' (ASAP) is a very common phrase in professional communication.

words.simpleMeaning: prepared and available to do something

sentences.daily: I am ready to leave.
sentences.office: Is the report ready for review?
sentences.interview: I am ready to take on new responsibilities.
sentences.business: Our product is ready for launch.
sentences.formal: The team is ready to begin operations.
sentences.informal: Are you ready? Everyone is waiting for you!

words.usageNote: 'Ready to do' something (infinitive). 'Get ready' = prepare. 'Ready-made' = already prepared. 'Ready, steady, go!' is a common sports phrase.

words.simpleMeaning: costing nothing, or not restricted

sentences.daily: Are you free tomorrow evening?
sentences.office: We have a free workshop on Friday.
sentences.interview: I am free to join from the 1st of next month.
sentences.business: We offer a free trial for new customers.
sentences.formal: Citizens are free to express their opinions.
sentences.informal: Free food at the party — I am definitely going!

words.usageNote: 'Free' as adjective means available or no cost. 'Free of charge' = at no cost. 'Feel free to' = please go ahead and do something.

words.simpleMeaning: easy to understand or do, not complicated

sentences.daily: This is a simple recipe.
sentences.office: Let me explain it in simple terms.
sentences.interview: I break complex problems into simple steps.
sentences.business: We keep our processes simple and effective.
sentences.formal: The solution is simple yet effective.
sentences.informal: It is simple! Just press that button!

words.usageNote: 'Simple' often means uncomplicated. 'Simply' as adverb: 'I simply do not agree.' 'Plain and simple' means very clear and obvious.

words.simpleMeaning: of high quality, pleasant, or useful

sentences.daily: I had a good day today.
sentences.office: She does good work consistently.
sentences.interview: I have good analytical and communication skills.
sentences.business: We maintain good relations with all clients.
sentences.formal: The project delivered good results.
sentences.informal: That was so good — I loved every minute!

words.usageNote: Comparative: better; Superlative: best. 'Good' is an adjective. 'Well' is the adverb: 'She speaks well' (not 'good'). 'Feel good' = feel happy/healthy.

words.simpleMeaning: recently made, found, or started

sentences.daily: I bought a new phone last week.
sentences.office: We welcomed a new team member today.
sentences.interview: I am eager to learn new technologies.
sentences.business: We launch new products every quarter.
sentences.formal: The new policy takes effect from January.
sentences.informal: Have you seen his new house? It is beautiful!

words.usageNote: 'New' comes before the noun: 'a new car'. 'Brand new' emphasizes it is completely new. 'New to' something means recently started: 'I am new to this job.'

words.simpleMeaning: the period during which something happens

sentences.daily: What is the time?
sentences.office: We need to manage our time efficiently.
sentences.interview: I deliver projects on time, every time.
sentences.business: Time is money in business.
sentences.formal: The time allocated for this session is 30 minutes.
sentences.informal: I have not had time to breathe today — so busy!

words.usageNote: 'On time' = punctual (not late). 'In time' = before the deadline. 'At the same time' = simultaneously. 'From time to time' = occasionally.

words.simpleMeaning: a particular area, position, or location

sentences.daily: This is a nice place to live.
sentences.office: Please place your bag in the designated area.
sentences.interview: Tell me about your previous place of work.
sentences.business: We place orders with verified suppliers.
sentences.formal: The event takes place on the 15th of this month.
sentences.informal: This place has the best biryani in the city!

words.usageNote: 'In place' = ready/prepared. 'Take place' = happen. 'Place an order' = submit an order. 'Out of place' = not appropriate or suitable.

words.simpleMeaning: a group of people who are related

sentences.daily: My family is very supportive.
sentences.office: I prioritize both work and family.
sentences.interview: I come from a family of educators.
sentences.business: We treat our employees like family.
sentences.formal: The policy supports family welfare.
sentences.informal: Family first, always!

words.usageNote: 'Family' can be singular or plural: 'My family is' or 'My family are' (British). 'Start a family' = have children.

words.simpleMeaning: a person you like and spend time with

sentences.daily: I meet my friends every weekend.
sentences.office: My colleague is also my friend.
sentences.interview: I make friends easily in any environment.
sentences.business: Good business relationships are built on friendship.
sentences.formal: The two nations maintain friendly relations.
sentences.informal: He is my best friend from school days!

words.usageNote: 'Make friends' = develop friendships. 'Best friend' = closest friend. 'Friendly' = adjective meaning warm and pleasant.

words.simpleMeaning: a situation that is difficult to deal with

sentences.daily: I have a problem with my internet connection.
sentences.office: What is the problem with this code?
sentences.interview: I enjoy solving complex problems.
sentences.business: Every problem has a solution.
sentences.formal: The problem requires immediate attention.
sentences.informal: No problem! I will handle it right now.

words.usageNote: 'No problem' = it is okay, do not worry. 'Have a problem with' = object to something. 'Problem-solving' is a key skill employers look for.

words.simpleMeaning: a response to a question

sentences.daily: The answer is 42.
sentences.office: I do not have an answer right now.
sentences.interview: My answer to all your questions is yes.
sentences.business: The answer to customer needs is innovation.
sentences.formal: Please provide your answer in writing.
sentences.informal: I knew the answer but forgot it during the test!

words.usageNote: 'Answer' a question/phone/door. 'Answer to someone' = be responsible to. 'In answer to' = as a response to.

words.simpleMeaning: a sentence asking for information

sentences.daily: Do you have any questions?
sentences.office: I have a question about the new policy.
sentences.interview: This is a great question.
sentences.business: The question is how to reduce costs.
sentences.formal: The question was raised in parliament.
sentences.informal: Good question — I have no idea honestly!

words.usageNote: 'Ask a question', 'raise a question'. 'Question' as verb means to doubt: 'I question his motives.' 'Out of the question' = impossible.

words.simpleMeaning: coins and notes used to buy things

sentences.daily: I do not have enough money.
sentences.office: We save money by reducing waste.
sentences.interview: I am not motivated only by money.
sentences.business: Money management is critical in business.
sentences.formal: The fund allocates money to research projects.
sentences.informal: Money can not buy happiness, but it helps!

words.usageNote: 'Money' is uncountable — no 'a money' or 'monies'. Use 'some money', 'a lot of money', 'How much money'. 'Make money' = earn. 'Save money' = not spend.

words.simpleMeaning: a building where people live

sentences.daily: I live in a two-bedroom house.
sentences.office: She works from home.
sentences.interview: I am looking to move closer to the office.
sentences.business: The company houses its employees in nearby apartments.
sentences.formal: The organization provides housing for low-income families.
sentences.informal: Our house is small but it feels like home!

words.usageNote: 'House' = the building. 'Home' = the feeling/place you belong. 'House' as verb: 'This building houses 200 families.'

words.simpleMeaning: a set of printed pages in a cover

sentences.daily: I read one book every month.
sentences.office: Please book a meeting room for 2 PM.
sentences.interview: I read books on leadership and management.
sentences.business: I will book a flight for the client visit.
sentences.formal: The library books are available for public borrowing.
sentences.informal: This book is so good — I finished it in one day!

words.usageNote: 'Book' as noun = reading material. 'Book' as verb = reserve/schedule. 'Book a ticket', 'book a hotel'. 'Booked' = fully reserved.

words.simpleMeaning: the clear liquid we drink

sentences.daily: I drink 8 glasses of water daily.
sentences.office: Please keep water bottles on the table.
sentences.interview: May I have some water, please?
sentences.business: Water conservation is our CSR priority.
sentences.formal: Access to clean water is a basic right.
sentences.informal: I am so thirsty — where is the water?

words.usageNote: 'Water' is uncountable. Use 'a glass of water', 'some water'. As verb: 'water the plants'. 'Water down' = dilute or weaken.

words.simpleMeaning: things we eat to stay alive and healthy

sentences.daily: I love Indian food.
sentences.office: The office canteen serves good food.
sentences.interview: I have no dietary restrictions.
sentences.business: Our food company supplies to 500 restaurants.
sentences.formal: Food security is a national priority.
sentences.informal: The food here is incredible — must try!

words.usageNote: 'Food' is uncountable. Say 'some food', 'a lot of food'. 'Foods' is used when referring to types: 'healthy foods'. 'Fast food', 'street food', 'junk food' are common phrases.

words.simpleMeaning: a large and important town

sentences.daily: I live in a big city.
sentences.office: Our office is in the heart of the city.
sentences.interview: I am willing to relocate to any city.
sentences.business: We are expanding to tier-2 cities.
sentences.formal: The city administration plans new infrastructure.
sentences.informal: This city never sleeps — always something happening!

words.usageNote: 'City' is bigger than a 'town'. 'In the city' = in the urban area. 'City centre' (BrE) or 'downtown' (AmE) = the main commercial area.

words.simpleMeaning: a nation with its own government

sentences.daily: India is my country.
sentences.office: We work with clients from 20 countries.
sentences.interview: I am open to working in a different country.
sentences.business: The company operates in multiple countries.
sentences.formal: The country's GDP has grown significantly.
sentences.informal: I love my country — nothing beats India!

words.usageNote: 'Country' = a nation. 'The country' (without name) can also mean rural areas: 'I prefer living in the country.' 'Country' as adjective: 'country music', 'country road'.

words.simpleMeaning: a place where children learn

sentences.daily: My son goes to school every day.
sentences.office: I attended a government school.
sentences.interview: I topped my school in the board exams.
sentences.business: We partner with schools for skill development.
sentences.formal: The school provides free education to all students.
sentences.informal: School was the best time of my life!

words.usageNote: 'Go to school' (no article) = attend as a student. 'Go to the school' = visit the building. Same rule applies to hospital, college, church, prison.

words.simpleMeaning: a place where people work at desks

sentences.daily: I reach office at 9 AM.
sentences.office: The office is on the 5th floor.
sentences.interview: I am comfortable working in an open office.
sentences.business: Our head office is in Bangalore.
sentences.formal: The office maintains records of all transactions.
sentences.informal: Office gossip can be quite entertaining sometimes!

words.usageNote: 'In the office' = inside. 'At the office' = at the location. 'Office hours' = working hours. 'Out of office' (OOO) = away from work.

words.simpleMeaning: a building where sick people receive medical care

sentences.daily: My father is in hospital.
sentences.office: The office is near the city hospital.
sentences.interview: I worked in hospital administration for 2 years.
sentences.business: We supply medicines to government hospitals.
sentences.formal: The hospital treats over 500 patients daily.
sentences.informal: I hate going to the hospital — the smell alone!

words.usageNote: 'In hospital' (BrE, no article) = as a patient. 'In the hospital' (AmE). 'Go to hospital' = as a patient. 'Go to the hospital' = visit someone.

words.simpleMeaning: a place where goods are bought and sold

sentences.daily: I go to the market every Saturday.
sentences.office: We analyze the market before launching a product.
sentences.interview: I have experience in market research.
sentences.business: Our target market is young professionals.
sentences.formal: The stock market showed an upward trend.
sentences.informal: The vegetable market near my house is the cheapest!

words.usageNote: 'Market' as noun = place to buy/sell. 'Market' as verb = promote. 'Job market' = opportunities for employment. 'Market share' = percentage of sales.

words.simpleMeaning: a path for vehicles to travel on

sentences.daily: The road to my house is very busy.
sentences.office: The office is on MG Road.
sentences.interview: I am willing to go on road trips for client visits.
sentences.business: Road connectivity is important for logistics.
sentences.formal: The road construction will be completed in six months.
sentences.informal: The road was blocked — took forever to reach!

words.usageNote: 'Road' is for vehicles. 'Street' is lined with buildings. 'Path' is for walking. 'On the road' = traveling. 'Road to success' = the path/journey.

words.simpleMeaning: a vehicle that travels on rails

sentences.daily: I take the train to work.
sentences.office: The company trains new employees for one month.
sentences.interview: I trained junior staff in my previous role.
sentences.business: We train our sales team quarterly.
sentences.formal: The training program lasts three weeks.
sentences.informal: I missed the last train — had to take a cab!

words.usageNote: 'Train' as noun = vehicle. 'Train' as verb = to teach/develop skills. 'By train' = using train as transport. 'On the train' = inside the train.

words.simpleMeaning: a large vehicle that carries many passengers

sentences.daily: I take the bus to college.
sentences.office: The company provides a bus service.
sentences.interview: The office is accessible by bus.
sentences.business: We use bus advertising for brand promotion.
sentences.formal: Bus rapid transit improves urban mobility.
sentences.informal: The bus was so crowded — I could barely breathe!

words.usageNote: 'By bus' = using bus as transport. 'On the bus' = inside the bus. 'Bus stop' = where you wait. 'Miss the bus' = be too late (literal and figurative).

words.simpleMeaning: a four-wheeled motor vehicle

sentences.daily: My father drives a car.
sentences.office: I have a company car.
sentences.interview: I have a valid driving license and own car.
sentences.business: We lease cars for our executives.
sentences.formal: The car industry contributes to the economy.
sentences.informal: His new car is absolutely stunning — lucky guy!

words.usageNote: 'By car' = using car as transport. 'In the car' = inside. 'Car park' (BrE) or 'parking lot' (AmE). 'Drive a car', 'ride a bike', 'take a bus' — different verbs for different vehicles.

words.simpleMeaning: a device used to communicate by voice

sentences.daily: My phone battery is dead.
sentences.office: Please keep your phone on silent in meetings.
sentences.interview: Can I have your phone number for follow-up?
sentences.business: We communicate with clients over the phone.
sentences.formal: The phone conference is scheduled at 3 PM.
sentences.informal: Put your phone down and talk to me!

words.usageNote: 'Phone someone' = call them. 'Answer the phone' = receive a call. 'On the phone' = currently speaking. 'Smart phone' or 'mobile phone' = cell phone.

words.simpleMeaning: an electronic device for processing data

sentences.daily: I use a computer for everything.
sentences.office: Our office computers are updated regularly.
sentences.interview: I am proficient in computer skills.
sentences.business: We use computers for data management.
sentences.formal: Computer literacy is essential in modern workplaces.
sentences.informal: My computer crashed and I lost all my work — disaster!

words.usageNote: 'On the computer' = using it. 'Computer science' = the academic subject. 'Computer skills' = ability to use computers. 'Desktop/laptop' are types of computers.

words.simpleMeaning: a global network connecting computers worldwide

sentences.daily: I use the internet for everything.
sentences.office: Our office internet is very fast.
sentences.interview: I learned many skills from the internet.
sentences.business: We promote our business through the internet.
sentences.formal: Internet access is vital for digital inclusion.
sentences.informal: No internet at home today — worst day ever!

words.usageNote: 'On the internet' = using it. 'The Internet' (capital I) is preferred in formal writing. 'Internet connection', 'internet speed', 'internet banking' are common phrases.

words.simpleMeaning: a message sent electronically

sentences.daily: I check my email every morning.
sentences.office: Please send me the report by email.
sentences.interview: You can email me your portfolio.
sentences.business: We respond to all emails within 4 hours.
sentences.formal: Please refer to the email dated 5th January.
sentences.informal: Did you see the email I sent? Check it now!

words.usageNote: 'Send an email to someone', 'reply to an email'. 'Email address' = your unique electronic address. Both 'email' and 'e-mail' are correct spellings.

words.simpleMeaning: when people come together to discuss something

sentences.daily: I have three meetings today.
sentences.office: The meeting starts at 10 AM.
sentences.interview: This feels like a very pleasant meeting.
sentences.business: We hold weekly review meetings.
sentences.formal: The annual general meeting is on December 15.
sentences.informal: Another meeting? Can we not just send an email?

words.usageNote: 'Have a meeting', 'attend a meeting', 'hold a meeting', 'call a meeting'. 'Meeting room' = a room for meetings. 'Stand-up meeting' = short daily team meeting.

words.simpleMeaning: a written or spoken account of something

sentences.daily: I submit a daily report to my manager.
sentences.office: The report shows our monthly progress.
sentences.interview: I write detailed project reports.
sentences.business: Our quarterly report shows strong results.
sentences.formal: The committee submits an annual report.
sentences.informal: My report is due tomorrow and I have not started yet!

words.usageNote: 'Report to someone' = work under them. 'Write/submit a report'. 'Report card' = student grades. 'News report' = news story.

words.simpleMeaning: a planned piece of work with a goal

sentences.daily: I am working on a new project.
sentences.office: The project deadline is next Friday.
sentences.interview: I managed a large-scale project in my previous job.
sentences.business: We delivered the project ahead of schedule.
sentences.formal: The project has been approved by the board.
sentences.informal: This project is killing me — so much pressure!

words.usageNote: 'Work on a project', 'manage a project', 'complete a project'. 'Project management' is a key professional skill. 'Project' as verb = forecast/display.

words.simpleMeaning: a group of people working together

sentences.daily: I work in a team of 8.
sentences.office: My team is very cooperative.
sentences.interview: I am a strong team player.
sentences.business: Our teams collaborate across departments.
sentences.formal: The team presents its findings to the board.
sentences.informal: Our team won the cricket match — what a day!

words.usageNote: 'Team player' = someone who works well in a group. 'Team work' = working together. In British English, 'the team are' (plural verb) is common.

words.simpleMeaning: money paid regularly for work

sentences.daily: My salary is credited on the 25th.
sentences.office: The HR discusses salary during appraisals.
sentences.interview: My expected salary is 8 LPA.
sentences.business: Employee salaries are our biggest expense.
sentences.formal: The salary structure is reviewed annually.
sentences.informal: I need a salary hike — prices are going up!

words.usageNote: 'Salary' is a fixed monthly payment. 'Wages' are often paid weekly or daily. 'Pay raise'/'hike' = increase in salary. 'CTC' = cost to company in India.

words.simpleMeaning: knowledge gained from doing something

sentences.daily: I have a lot of experience cooking.
sentences.office: My 5 years of experience helps me a lot.
sentences.interview: I have experience in both sales and marketing.
sentences.business: Customer experience is our top priority.
sentences.formal: Candidates with relevant experience are preferred.
sentences.informal: Just dive in — experience is the best teacher!

words.usageNote: 'Work experience' = professional history. 'Experience' is usually uncountable: 'I have experience' (not 'an experience'). 'An experience' = a single event.

words.simpleMeaning: the ability to do something well

sentences.daily: Cooking is a useful skill.
sentences.office: My communication skills help in meetings.
sentences.interview: My key skills are problem-solving and teamwork.
sentences.business: We invest in employee skills development.
sentences.formal: The course enhances analytical skills.
sentences.informal: That move was pure skill — incredible!

words.usageNote: 'Hard skills' = technical skills (coding, accounting). 'Soft skills' = interpersonal skills (communication, teamwork). 'Skilled' = having the skill. 'Skill set' = all your skills.

words.simpleMeaning: a system of words used by people to communicate

sentences.daily: I speak three languages.
sentences.office: Our office communication is in English.
sentences.interview: I am fluent in English and conversational in Tamil.
sentences.business: We do business in multiple languages.
sentences.formal: Language proficiency is tested in the assessment.
sentences.informal: Learning a new language is the best thing I did!

words.usageNote: 'Speak a language', 'learn a language', 'know a language'. 'Mother tongue' = first language. 'Body language' = non-verbal communication. 'Language barrier' = difficulty due to different languages.

words.simpleMeaning: to gain knowledge or skill through study or practice

sentences.daily: I learn something new every day.
sentences.office: She learns new tools quickly.
sentences.interview: I love to learn and grow continuously.
sentences.business: Our team learns from every project.
sentences.formal: Students learn best with practical examples.
sentences.informal: You never learn, do you? This is the third time!

words.usageNote: Both 'learned' and 'learnt' are acceptable past forms. 'Learn by heart' = memorize. 'Learn from mistakes' = improve based on errors.

words.simpleMeaning: to give knowledge to someone

sentences.daily: My father teaches me good values.
sentences.office: She teaches new employees the procedures.
sentences.interview: I enjoy teaching and mentoring juniors.
sentences.business: We teach clients how to use our software.
sentences.formal: The professor teaches advanced economics.
sentences.informal: Let me teach you this trick — it is super useful!

words.usageNote: Irregular verb: teach → taught → taught. 'Teach someone something' or 'teach someone how to'. 'Teacher' = person who teaches.

words.simpleMeaning: to spend time learning about a subject

sentences.daily: I study for two hours every evening.
sentences.office: She studied engineering before joining finance.
sentences.interview: I studied MBA with a focus on marketing.
sentences.business: We study market trends carefully.
sentences.formal: The research team studies consumer behavior.
sentences.informal: I need to study but Netflix is calling me!

words.usageNote: 'Study' focuses on deliberate learning. 'Study at' a university. 'Study for' an exam. 'Area of study' = academic subject.

words.simpleMeaning: work done for money on a regular basis

sentences.daily: I love my job.
sentences.office: What is your job here?
sentences.interview: I am looking for a job that challenges me.
sentences.business: Job creation is our commitment to society.
sentences.formal: The job description is available on our website.
sentences.informal: Quit your job if it is making you unhappy!

words.usageNote: 'Do a good job' = perform well. 'Job well done!' = excellent work. 'Job market' = availability of jobs. 'Job seeker' = person looking for employment.

words.simpleMeaning: an organization that sells products or services

sentences.daily: I work for a software company.
sentences.office: Our company has 500 employees.
sentences.interview: I want to join your company because of its culture.
sentences.business: The company achieved record profits.
sentences.formal: The company files its annual returns in April.
sentences.informal: Company parties are always the highlight of the year!

words.usageNote: 'Work for a company' or 'work at a company'. 'Company' also means companionship: 'I enjoy your company.' 'Keep someone company' = be with them.

words.simpleMeaning: the activity of buying and selling goods or services

sentences.daily: Business is going well.
sentences.office: It is business as usual today.
sentences.interview: I want to start my own business someday.
sentences.business: Business growth depends on customer satisfaction.
sentences.formal: The business submitted its audit report.
sentences.informal: Mind your own business — stop interfering!

words.usageNote: 'Do business with' = have commercial dealings. 'Business hours' = working hours. 'Business casual' = semi-formal dress code. 'None of your business' = private matter.

words.simpleMeaning: a person who buys goods or services

sentences.daily: The customer is always right.
sentences.office: We received great feedback from the customer.
sentences.interview: I have experience in customer service.
sentences.business: Customer satisfaction is our top priority.
sentences.formal: The company handles over 10,000 customers.
sentences.informal: That customer was so difficult to deal with today!

words.usageNote: 'Customer' = general buyer. 'Client' = usually a professional/business relationship. 'Customer service' = help provided to buyers. 'Loyal customer' = returns regularly.

words.simpleMeaning: something made to be sold

sentences.daily: I bought a great product online.
sentences.office: Our new product launches next month.
sentences.interview: I managed a product from concept to launch.
sentences.business: Product quality differentiates us from competitors.
sentences.formal: The product meets all regulatory standards.
sentences.informal: This product is amazing — totally worth the price!

words.usageNote: 'Product' = physical goods. 'Service' = intangible offering. 'Product launch' = introduction of a new product. 'Product manager' = person who oversees a product.

words.simpleMeaning: the amount of money needed to buy something

sentences.daily: What is the price of this shirt?
sentences.office: We need to price our service competitively.
sentences.interview: I handled pricing strategy in my previous role.
sentences.business: Price determines the market position.
sentences.formal: The price list is available on request.
sentences.informal: The price is way too high — let us negotiate!

words.usageNote: 'Price' is set by the seller. 'Cost' is what you pay. 'Value' = what something is worth. 'Price tag' = label showing price. 'At any price' = no matter the cost.

words.simpleMeaning: how good or bad something is

sentences.daily: This restaurant has good quality food.
sentences.office: Quality control is done at every step.
sentences.interview: I believe in quality over quantity.
sentences.business: Quality is what keeps customers coming back.
sentences.formal: The product meets international quality standards.
sentences.informal: This is such bad quality — never buying from them again!

words.usageNote: 'Quality' can mean high standard (adjective): 'quality products'. 'Quality control' = checking that standards are met. 'Quality time' = meaningful time spent.

words.simpleMeaning: a thought or suggestion about what to do

sentences.daily: I have an idea for the weekend.
sentences.office: She always comes up with great ideas.
sentences.interview: I proposed an idea that saved the company time.
sentences.business: Ideas drive innovation.
sentences.formal: The idea was approved by the committee.
sentences.informal: Brilliant idea! Why did we not think of this before?

words.usageNote: 'Have an idea', 'get an idea', 'come up with an idea'. 'Good idea!' = I agree. 'No idea' = I do not know. 'Idea' is countable: 'one idea, two ideas'.

words.simpleMeaning: a choice made after thinking about it

sentences.daily: It was a difficult decision.
sentences.office: The manager makes all key decisions.
sentences.interview: I make data-driven decisions.
sentences.business: Every business decision has consequences.
sentences.formal: The decision was made unanimously.
sentences.informal: You made the right decision — trust yourself!

words.usageNote: 'Make a decision' = decide. 'Final decision' = the last and binding choice. 'Decision-maker' = person in authority. 'Good decision!' = well chosen.

words.simpleMeaning: achieving a goal or desired outcome

sentences.daily: Hard work leads to success.
sentences.office: We celebrate every success as a team.
sentences.interview: My biggest success was leading a 20-member team.
sentences.business: Business success requires consistent effort.
sentences.formal: The project was a great success.
sentences.informal: I knew you would succeed — no doubt in my mind!

words.usageNote: 'Success' is uncountable in general: 'wish you success'. Countable as specific event: 'a great success'. 'Successful' = adjective. 'Succeed' = verb.

words.simpleMeaning: to not succeed in doing something

sentences.daily: I failed my driving test the first time.
sentences.office: Never fail to inform the manager of issues.
sentences.interview: I learned more from failures than successes.
sentences.business: Most startups fail in the first year.
sentences.formal: The system failed due to a software error.
sentences.informal: I failed the exam but it is not the end of the world!

words.usageNote: 'Fail to do' = not manage to do. 'Fail an exam' = not pass. 'Failure' = the noun. 'Epic fail' (informal) = complete failure. 'Without fail' = always/certainly.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling or showing pleasure or contentment

sentences.daily: I am happy to see you.
sentences.office: We are happy to announce a new project.
sentences.interview: I am happy to work in any department.
sentences.business: Happy customers become loyal customers.
sentences.formal: We are happy to confirm your appointment.
sentences.informal: I am SO happy right now — best day ever!

words.usageNote: 'Happy to do' = glad/willing. 'Happy about/with' = satisfied with a situation. 'Happiness' = the noun. 'Happily' = the adverb.

words.simpleMeaning: not easy, needs a lot of effort

sentences.daily: English grammar is difficult at first.
sentences.office: This is a difficult task but I can do it.
sentences.interview: Tell me about a difficult situation you faced.
sentences.business: Difficult times test your true character.
sentences.formal: The project presents several difficult challenges.
sentences.informal: It is really difficult to wake up on Monday mornings!

words.usageNote: 'Difficult to do' or 'difficult for someone to do'. 'Have difficulty doing' = find it hard. 'Difficulty' = noun. 'Difficulties' = plural problems/challenges.

words.simpleMeaning: not difficult, needs little effort

sentences.daily: This recipe is very easy.
sentences.office: The new software is easy to use.
sentences.interview: I make complex processes easy to understand.
sentences.business: We make banking easy for customers.
sentences.formal: The system provides easy access to information.
sentences.informal: Easy! I finished it in 10 minutes!

words.usageNote: 'Easy to do', 'take it easy' = relax, 'easier said than done' = sounds simple but is actually hard. 'Easily' = adverb.

words.simpleMeaning: moving or doing things quickly

sentences.daily: Please speak fast — I am getting late.
sentences.office: She works fast without compromising quality.
sentences.interview: I am a fast learner.
sentences.business: Fast delivery is our competitive advantage.
sentences.formal: The fast-growing company employs 1000 people.
sentences.informal: That car is insanely fast — must be a sports car!

words.usageNote: 'Fast' as adjective: 'a fast car'. 'Fast' as adverb: 'She runs fast'. Note: 'quickly' is also an adverb but 'fastly' is NOT a word.

words.simpleMeaning: moving or doing things not quickly

sentences.daily: The internet is very slow today.
sentences.office: Business is slow in December.
sentences.interview: I prefer slow and steady over fast and careless.
sentences.business: We are taking slow but steady steps.
sentences.formal: Economic growth has slowed down.
sentences.informal: Why are you so slow today? Everything okay?

words.usageNote: 'Slow down' = reduce speed. 'Slowly' = adverb: 'Please speak slowly.' 'Slow' as adjective: 'a slow process'. 'Go slow' = deliberately work slowly.

words.simpleMeaning: having great physical power or firm determination

sentences.daily: My father is physically very strong.
sentences.office: She has strong communication skills.
sentences.interview: I have a strong background in data analysis.
sentences.business: We have a strong brand presence.
sentences.formal: The strong economic indicators are encouraging.
sentences.informal: That argument is very strong — hard to disagree!

words.usageNote: 'Strong' for physical and metaphorical power. 'Strength' = noun. 'Strongly' = adverb: 'I strongly believe'. 'Go from strength to strength' = keep improving.

words.simpleMeaning: to make things easier for someone

sentences.daily: Can you help me move this table?
sentences.office: I help my colleagues whenever needed.
sentences.interview: I helped my team increase sales by 20%.
sentences.business: We help businesses grow through technology.
sentences.formal: The government helps citizens through welfare programs.
sentences.informal: You are such a great help — I owe you one!

words.usageNote: Already covered. Adding for reinforcement: 'Help yourself' = take what you need. 'Can not help doing' = cannot stop doing something. 'With the help of' = using assistance.

words.simpleMeaning: a strong feeling of affection, or to like very much

sentences.daily: I love my family.
sentences.office: I love what I do for a living.
sentences.interview: I love challenges that help me grow.
sentences.business: We love hearing from our customers.
sentences.formal: The program fosters a culture of love for learning.
sentences.informal: I absolutely love this place — we should come again!

words.usageNote: 'Love doing' = enjoy an activity. 'Love someone' = feel deep affection. 'Love it!' (informal) = I really like this. 'Love' is a state verb — don't use 'I am loving it' (incorrect in standard English).

words.simpleMeaning: to strongly dislike something or someone

sentences.daily: I hate waking up late.
sentences.office: Nobody likes conflicts — we all hate them.
sentences.interview: I hate to say this, but I left for personal reasons.
sentences.business: Customers hate poor service.
sentences.formal: The policy aims to eliminate hatred in workplaces.
sentences.informal: I hate Mondays so much — does anyone like them?!

words.usageNote: 'Hate doing' or 'hate to do' — both acceptable. 'Hate' is a state verb — avoid 'I am hating it'. 'Hatred' = noun.

words.simpleMeaning: to feel a desire or wish for something

sentences.daily: I want a cup of tea.
sentences.office: What do you want to discuss?
sentences.interview: I want to grow as a professional.
sentences.business: Customers want value for their money.
sentences.formal: The organization wants dedicated professionals.
sentences.informal: I just want to sleep all day — is that too much?

words.usageNote: 'Want to do' = desire an action. 'Want someone to do' = desire someone else's action. 'Want' is a state verb — avoid 'I am wanting'. Use 'want' not 'I am wanting'.

words.simpleMeaning: to enjoy or find something pleasant

sentences.daily: I like cold coffee.
sentences.office: I like working independently.
sentences.interview: I like solving complex problems.
sentences.business: We like to exceed expectations.
sentences.formal: The report indicates that users like the new interface.
sentences.informal: I like this song so much — what is it called?

words.usageNote: 'Like doing' and 'like to do' both work: 'I like swimming' / 'I like to swim'. 'Like' in comparison: 'She sings like a professional.'

words.simpleMeaning: at all times, every time

sentences.daily: I always wake up at 6 AM.
sentences.office: She always submits work on time.
sentences.interview: I always maintain a positive attitude.
sentences.business: We always put the customer first.
sentences.formal: The team always follows safety protocols.
sentences.informal: He is always late — it is so annoying!

words.usageNote: Frequency adverbs order: subject + always/usually/often/sometimes/rarely/never + verb. 'I always eat breakfast' (not 'I eat always breakfast').

words.simpleMeaning: not at any time, not ever

sentences.daily: I never skip breakfast.
sentences.office: She never misses a deadline.
sentences.interview: I never give up easily.
sentences.business: We never compromise on quality.
sentences.formal: The organization never discriminates on any basis.
sentences.informal: I will never understand why people do that!

words.usageNote: 'Never' is negative — do not use with another negative: 'I never see nobody' is wrong. Say 'I never see anybody.' Place 'never' before the main verb.

words.simpleMeaning: on some occasions, not always

sentences.daily: I sometimes work from home.
sentences.office: Sometimes deadlines change at the last minute.
sentences.interview: I sometimes work overtime to meet targets.
sentences.business: Sometimes customer needs surprise us.
sentences.formal: Sometimes exceptions can be made.
sentences.informal: Sometimes I wonder what I am doing with my life!

words.usageNote: 'Sometimes' can go at the beginning or end of a sentence too: 'Sometimes I exercise.' or 'I exercise sometimes.' More flexible than other frequency adverbs.

words.simpleMeaning: many times, frequently

sentences.daily: I often go for a walk in the evening.
sentences.office: She often volunteers for extra work.
sentences.interview: I often present to senior management.
sentences.business: We often get referrals from happy customers.
sentences.formal: The committee often reviews ongoing projects.
sentences.informal: How often do you eat out? We should go together!

words.usageNote: The 't' in 'often' is silent in many accents: /ˈɒfən/. 'How often?' = asking about frequency. 'More often than not' = usually.

words.simpleMeaning: used to refer to all members of a group

sentences.daily: I exercise every morning.
sentences.office: Every Monday we have a team meeting.
sentences.interview: I give every task my full attention.
sentences.business: Every customer matters to us.
sentences.formal: Every employee must comply with the code of conduct.
sentences.informal: I eat ice cream every single day — no regrets!

words.usageNote: 'Every' takes singular verb: 'Every student is responsible'. 'Every day' (two words) = daily. 'Everyday' (one word) = ordinary/regular. These are different!

words.simpleMeaning: this current day

sentences.daily: Today is Monday.
sentences.office: We have a client visit today.
sentences.interview: Today I want to share my experience.
sentences.business: Today's markets are very competitive.
sentences.formal: As of today, the policy is in effect.
sentences.informal: Today is my lucky day — everything worked out!

words.usageNote: 'Today' at the beginning adds emphasis. 'Today's' = of today. 'Nowadays' = in the current period (broader than 'today'). 'Today or tomorrow' = very soon.

words.simpleMeaning: the day after today

sentences.daily: I have a dentist appointment tomorrow.
sentences.office: Tomorrow is the deadline for the project.
sentences.interview: I can join from tomorrow if needed.
sentences.business: Tomorrow will bring new opportunities.
sentences.formal: The decision will be announced tomorrow.
sentences.informal: I will do it tomorrow — the classic procrastinator's motto!

words.usageNote: 'Tomorrow morning/afternoon/evening'. 'Tomorrow's meeting' = the meeting scheduled for tomorrow. 'Tomorrow never comes' = a warning against procrastination.

words.simpleMeaning: the day before today

sentences.daily: Yesterday was a holiday.
sentences.office: I submitted the report yesterday.
sentences.interview: Yesterday I attended a seminar on AI.
sentences.business: Yesterday's data shows a drop in sales.
sentences.formal: The meeting held yesterday concluded successfully.
sentences.informal: Yesterday was the worst day — everything went wrong!

words.usageNote: Always used with past tense. 'Yesterday morning/afternoon/evening/night'. 'Day before yesterday' = two days ago.

words.simpleMeaning: the early part of the day, before noon

sentences.daily: Good morning! Have a great day.
sentences.office: Morning meetings are most productive.
sentences.interview: I prefer morning interviews — I am at my best.
sentences.business: Morning is the best time for important calls.
sentences.formal: The morning session begins at 9 AM.
sentences.informal: Not a morning person? Same! I need my coffee first.

words.usageNote: 'In the morning' = during morning hours. 'This morning' = today morning. 'Good morning!' = greeting. 'Early morning' = before sunrise.

words.simpleMeaning: the end of the afternoon and beginning of night

sentences.daily: I relax in the evening.
sentences.office: The evening shift starts at 5 PM.
sentences.interview: I am also available for an evening interview.
sentences.business: Evening networking events are common.
sentences.formal: Good evening, ladies and gentlemen.
sentences.informal: Evening plans? Want to grab some coffee?

words.usageNote: 'In the evening'. 'Good evening' = formal greeting after 6 PM. 'Evening wear' = formal clothes for night events. 'This evening' = tonight.

words.simpleMeaning: a period of seven days

sentences.daily: I work five days a week.
sentences.office: This week has been very productive.
sentences.interview: I can join within a week.
sentences.business: We review targets every week.
sentences.formal: The program runs for six weeks.
sentences.informal: This week is flying by — almost Friday already!

words.usageNote: 'A week' = 7 days. 'Last week', 'this week', 'next week'. 'Weekly' = happening every week. 'Weekday' = Mon-Fri. 'Weekend' = Sat-Sun.

words.simpleMeaning: a period of approximately 30 days

sentences.daily: My salary comes every month.
sentences.office: We submit a monthly report.
sentences.interview: I can join within a month.
sentences.business: Monthly targets are set every quarter.
sentences.formal: The contract is for a period of 12 months.
sentences.informal: I cannot believe it has been a month already!

words.usageNote: 'This month', 'last month', 'next month'. 'Monthly' = every month. 'A month's notice' = one month of notice before leaving a job.

words.simpleMeaning: a period of 12 months or 365 days

sentences.daily: I have been learning English for one year.
sentences.office: This year has been challenging.
sentences.interview: I have 4 years of experience.
sentences.business: Yearly revenue has grown by 25%.
sentences.formal: The financial year ends in March.
sentences.informal: This year has gone so fast — I cannot believe it!

words.usageNote: 'This year', 'last year', 'next year'. 'Years ago' = in the past. 'Yearly'/'annual' = once a year. 'Per year'/'per annum' (p.a.) = rate per year.

words.simpleMeaning: a symbol or word representing an amount

sentences.daily: What is your phone number?
sentences.office: Please note down this reference number.
sentences.interview: Can I have your contact number?
sentences.business: Our sales numbers are impressive.
sentences.formal: A number of issues need to be addressed.
sentences.informal: I lost your number — can you give it again?

words.usageNote: 'A number of' = several/many (plural). 'The number of' = a specific count (singular). 'Number one' = the best/most important.

words.simpleMeaning: the word used to identify a person or thing

sentences.daily: My name is Arjun.
sentences.office: Can I know your name, please?
sentences.interview: My full name is Arjun Kumar Sharma.
sentences.business: Our brand name is well-recognized.
sentences.formal: Please write your name in capital letters.
sentences.informal: What is your name? I keep forgetting!

words.usageNote: 'What is your name?' / 'My name is...'. 'First name' = given name. 'Last name/surname' = family name. 'In someone's name' = belonging to or on behalf of them.

words.simpleMeaning: the location of a building, or to speak to someone formally

sentences.daily: What is your home address?
sentences.office: Please address the envelope correctly.
sentences.interview: My current address is in Noida.
sentences.business: The CEO addressed the annual conference.
sentences.formal: Kindly address any complaints to the manager.
sentences.informal: I do not know his address — should have saved it!

words.usageNote: 'Home address', 'email address', 'office address'. 'Address someone' = speak to them formally. 'Address an issue' = deal with it.

words.simpleMeaning: the number of years someone has lived

sentences.daily: My age is 25.
sentences.office: Employees of all ages work here.
sentences.interview: I am 27 years of age.
sentences.business: The product targets the 25-35 age group.
sentences.formal: Age is not a barrier for this position.
sentences.informal: I cannot believe your age — you look so young!

words.usageNote: 'What is your age?' or 'How old are you?' are both correct. 'Age group' = range. 'Come of age' = become an adult. 'Middle age' = approximately 40-60.

words.simpleMeaning: the condition of your body and mind

sentences.daily: Health is wealth.
sentences.office: Our company provides health insurance.
sentences.interview: I maintain good health through exercise.
sentences.business: Employee health is our priority.
sentences.formal: Good health supports better productivity.
sentences.informal: Take care of your health first — nothing else matters!

words.usageNote: 'Good/poor health'. 'In good health' = healthy. 'Health insurance' = medical coverage. 'Public health' = community health management. 'Health-conscious' = aware of health.

words.simpleMeaning: a person trained to treat illness

sentences.daily: I need to see a doctor.
sentences.office: My colleague is a doctor of philosophy.
sentences.interview: I worked in a doctor's clinic.
sentences.business: Doctors are our target customers.
sentences.formal: The doctor diagnosed the patient with pneumonia.
sentences.informal: The doctor said no sweets — my worst nightmare!

words.usageNote: 'See a doctor' = have a medical appointment. 'Doctor of Philosophy' (PhD) is also called 'doctor'. 'Doctor's orders' = strict medical advice.

words.simpleMeaning: a person who teaches others

sentences.daily: My English teacher is excellent.
sentences.office: I am a teacher by profession.
sentences.interview: My previous role involved training — almost like a teacher.
sentences.business: Teachers need better tools and resources.
sentences.formal: The teacher addressed the students at the assembly.
sentences.informal: He is not just a teacher — he is a life coach!

words.usageNote: 'Teacher' = general. 'Professor' = university level. 'Tutor' = private teacher. 'Instructor' = someone who teaches a skill (driving instructor).

words.simpleMeaning: a person who studies at a school or university

sentences.daily: I am a student of engineering.
sentences.office: Student interns can apply for full-time roles.
sentences.interview: As a student, I won several competitions.
sentences.business: Students are a major customer segment.
sentences.formal: The student submitted her thesis on time.
sentences.informal: Student life is the best — no responsibilities!

words.usageNote: 'Student' is for secondary and higher education. 'Pupil' is for primary school (especially BrE). 'Student visa' = visa for studying abroad.

words.simpleMeaning: a person who designs or builds technical things

sentences.daily: My brother is an engineer.
sentences.office: She works as a software engineer.
sentences.interview: I am a mechanical engineer with 3 years experience.
sentences.business: We need to hire more engineers.
sentences.formal: The engineer submitted the technical specifications.
sentences.informal: Engineers get all the best job offers!

words.usageNote: Types: software engineer, civil engineer, mechanical engineer, electrical engineer. 'Engineering' = the field. 'Engineer' as verb: 'to engineer a solution' = to design/create.

words.simpleMeaning: a person who supervises a team or business

sentences.daily: My manager is very supportive.
sentences.office: The manager reviews all reports.
sentences.interview: I report directly to the project manager.
sentences.business: Effective managers inspire their teams.
sentences.formal: The manager approved the budget proposal.
sentences.informal: My manager actually praised me today — shocked!

words.usageNote: 'Line manager' = direct supervisor. 'Project manager' = leads a specific project. 'Senior manager' = more senior role. 'Management' = the group of managers.

words.simpleMeaning: the group of people who control a country

sentences.daily: The government provides free education.
sentences.office: We supply to government departments.
sentences.interview: I have experience working with government clients.
sentences.business: Government policies affect business growth.
sentences.formal: The government announced a new scheme.
sentences.informal: The government always has too many rules!

words.usageNote: In BrE, 'the government are' (plural) is common. In AmE, 'the government is' (singular). 'Government job' = public sector employment.

words.simpleMeaning: facts or details about something

sentences.daily: I need more information about this.
sentences.office: Please share the information with the team.
sentences.interview: I provide accurate information at all times.
sentences.business: Information is the most valuable asset today.
sentences.formal: For further information, contact our helpdesk.
sentences.informal: Got any information about this? Tell me everything!

words.usageNote: 'Information' is uncountable — no 'an information' or 'informations'. Use 'a piece of information'. 'Information technology' (IT) = technology for managing data.

words.simpleMeaning: recently received or noteworthy information

sentences.daily: I watch the news every morning.
sentences.office: Any news about the project approval?
sentences.interview: I keep up with industry news.
sentences.business: Business news impacts market decisions.
sentences.formal: The news release was published yesterday.
sentences.informal: Have you heard the news? It is unbelievable!

words.usageNote: 'News' is uncountable — 'the news is', not 'the news are'. 'Good news' / 'bad news'. 'Break the news' = tell someone about something unexpected.

words.simpleMeaning: sounds arranged in a pleasing way

sentences.daily: I listen to music while working.
sentences.office: Background music makes the office pleasant.
sentences.interview: Music is my hobby — I play the guitar.
sentences.business: The music industry has moved to streaming.
sentences.formal: The concert featured classical music.
sentences.informal: This music is so addictive — I love it!

words.usageNote: 'Music' is uncountable. 'A piece of music' = one composition. 'Listen to music', 'play music'. 'Musical' = adjective. 'Musician' = person who plays music.

words.simpleMeaning: a physical activity done for exercise or competition

sentences.daily: Cricket is my favourite sport.
sentences.office: Sport builds teamwork skills.
sentences.interview: I was the captain of my college cricket team.
sentences.business: Sports sponsorship increases brand awareness.
sentences.formal: Sport promotes physical and mental health.
sentences.informal: Which sport do you follow? I am a huge cricket fan!

words.usageNote: In AmE: 'sports' (plural) is more common: 'I play sports'. In BrE: 'sport' (singular) is also used: 'I play sport'. 'Sportsman/sportswoman' = person who plays sport.

words.simpleMeaning: to go from one place to another

sentences.daily: I love to travel.
sentences.office: Do you need to travel for this role?
sentences.interview: I am comfortable with travel requirements.
sentences.business: Business travel is part of my current role.
sentences.formal: Travel expenses will be reimbursed.
sentences.informal: Travel is the best education you can get!

words.usageNote: British: 'travelled', 'travelling'. American: 'traveled', 'traveling'. 'Travel' is both noun and verb. 'Traveller' = person who travels.

words.simpleMeaning: very attractive or pleasing

sentences.daily: What a beautiful day!
sentences.office: She gave a beautiful presentation.
sentences.interview: India is a beautiful country with diverse culture.
sentences.business: Beautiful packaging attracts customers.
sentences.formal: The garden features beautiful landscaping.
sentences.informal: That sunset was absolutely beautiful — I took 100 photos!

words.usageNote: 'Beautiful' for things that appeal to senses. 'Beautifully' = adverb. 'Beauty' = noun. 'Beauty is in the eye of the beholder' = everyone finds different things beautiful.

words.simpleMeaning: free from dirt, or to remove dirt

sentences.daily: Please keep the office clean.
sentences.office: The cleaning team comes at 8 AM.
sentences.interview: I maintain a clean and organized workspace.
sentences.business: Clean energy is the future.
sentences.formal: The facility maintains clean and safe conditions.
sentences.informal: My room is clean for once — shocking, right?!

words.usageNote: 'Clean up' = tidy a mess. 'Clean out' = remove everything. 'Clean record' = no criminal history. 'Clean slate' = fresh start.

words.simpleMeaning: having existed for a long time

sentences.daily: This house is very old.
sentences.office: The old system is being replaced.
sentences.interview: I have old experience but I keep updating my skills.
sentences.business: Old customers are more loyal than new ones.
sentences.formal: The old policy has been revised.
sentences.informal: Oh come on, that joke is so old — everybody knows it!

words.usageNote: Comparative: older/elder. Superlative: oldest/eldest. 'Elder' and 'eldest' are used for family members only. 'Old' for things and age.

words.simpleMeaning: having lived for only a short time

sentences.daily: She looks very young for her age.
sentences.office: Young professionals bring fresh ideas.
sentences.interview: Though young, I have strong skills.
sentences.business: Targeting young consumers drives growth.
sentences.formal: The program develops young leaders.
sentences.informal: You are too young to understand this — trust me!

words.usageNote: Comparative: younger. Superlative: youngest. 'The young' = young people as a group. 'Young at heart' = feeling young despite age.

words.simpleMeaning: large in size, extent, or importance

sentences.daily: I have a big family.
sentences.office: We have a big client meeting tomorrow.
sentences.interview: My biggest achievement was managing a large team.
sentences.business: Thinking big is essential for growth.
