sentences.formal: The big decision will be made next week.
sentences.informal: This is a BIG deal — do not mess it up!

words.usageNote: Comparative: bigger. Superlative: biggest. 'Big' and 'large' are often interchangeable but 'big' is more informal. 'Big deal' = something important (or sarcastic).

words.simpleMeaning: little in size or amount

sentences.daily: I live in a small apartment.
sentences.office: Our startup is a small but growing company.
sentences.interview: Even small tasks deserve full attention.
sentences.business: Small businesses drive the economy.
sentences.formal: The project involves a small team of specialists.
sentences.informal: It is a small world! I bumped into him in London!

words.usageNote: Comparative: smaller. Superlative: smallest. 'Small talk' = light casual conversation. 'Small print/fine print' = details in a contract.

words.simpleMeaning: correct, or the direction opposite to left

sentences.daily: Turn right at the traffic light.
sentences.office: You are right — we need a new approach.
sentences.interview: The right attitude matters more than experience.
sentences.business: Right decisions lead to right results.
sentences.formal: Every citizen has the right to vote.
sentences.informal: Right! Let us stop arguing and get it done.

words.usageNote: 'Right' = correct. 'Right' = direction. 'Right' = a legal entitlement. 'All right' = okay/acceptable. 'Right away' = immediately.

words.simpleMeaning: not correct, not right

sentences.daily: That answer is wrong.
sentences.office: Something is wrong with this report.
sentences.interview: I admit when I am wrong and correct myself.
sentences.business: Wrong data leads to wrong decisions.
sentences.formal: The decision was wrong on both moral and legal grounds.
sentences.informal: You have the wrong number! This is not a pizza place!

words.usageNote: 'Wrong' as adjective: 'That is wrong.' 'Wrong' as adverb: 'He did it wrong.' 'Go wrong' = become bad. 'What is wrong?' = what is the problem?

words.simpleMeaning: great in length, distance, or time

sentences.daily: It is a long drive to my hometown.
sentences.office: The meeting was very long today.
sentences.interview: I have a long-term career plan.
sentences.business: Long-term relationships build trust.
sentences.formal: The document is 100 pages long.
sentences.informal: That was the longest queue I have ever stood in!

words.usageNote: Comparative: longer. Superlative: longest. 'Long' for distance and time. 'For a long time' = over many years. 'Long for' = to want deeply.

words.simpleMeaning: small in height or brief in time

sentences.daily: The meeting was short and effective.
sentences.office: I need a short break.
sentences.interview: In short, I am a quick learner.
sentences.business: Short-term goals support long-term success.
sentences.formal: Please submit a short summary.
sentences.informal: To cut a long story short — he left the company!

words.usageNote: Comparative: shorter. Superlative: shortest. 'Short of' = lacking. 'Short cut' = a quicker route. 'In short' = to summarize.

words.simpleMeaning: at a great distance above the ground, or great in amount

sentences.daily: The price is too high.
sentences.office: We maintain high standards.
sentences.interview: I set high goals for myself.
sentences.business: High quality products command premium prices.
sentences.formal: The high court delivered its judgment.
sentences.informal: Prices are at an all-time high — everything is so expensive!

words.usageNote: 'High' = elevated. 'Tall' = used for people and buildings. Do not say 'a high man' — say 'a tall man'. 'High in' vitamins/calories etc. = containing much of.

words.simpleMeaning: below normal height or amount

sentences.daily: The battery is low.
sentences.office: Our error rate is very low.
sentences.interview: I handle both high and low pressure situations well.
sentences.business: Low cost does not mean low quality for us.
sentences.formal: The low unemployment rate indicates economic growth.
sentences.informal: My energy is at an all-time low today — need coffee!

words.usageNote: Comparative: lower. Superlative: lowest. 'Low' for amount/level/quantity. 'Feel low' = feel sad. 'Lay low' = stay hidden. 'Low-key' = quiet/modest.

words.simpleMeaning: to express gratitude to someone

sentences.daily: Thank you for your help.
sentences.office: I thank my team for their hard work.
sentences.interview: Thank you for this opportunity.
sentences.business: We thank our clients for their trust.
sentences.formal: The organization thanks all volunteers.
sentences.informal: Thanks a million! You are the best!

words.usageNote: 'Thank you' = formal. 'Thanks' = informal. 'Thank you very much', 'many thanks'. 'Thanks to' = because of. 'Grateful' and 'thankful' = adjectives expressing thanks.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling regret or apology for something

sentences.daily: I am sorry for being late.
sentences.office: I am sorry for the inconvenience.
sentences.interview: Sorry, could you repeat the question?
sentences.business: We are sorry to hear about your experience.
sentences.formal: We sincerely apologize for any inconvenience caused.
sentences.informal: Sorry! Did not mean to bump into you!

words.usageNote: 'Sorry for' + noun/gerund: 'Sorry for the delay.' 'Sorry about' + noun/situation: 'Sorry about that.' 'I am sorry to hear that' = expression of sympathy.
